@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]
input.new_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, ...]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can pass ``with_counts=True`` to also get the number of items in each group, without needing ``|list|length`` in the template. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.2 Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) groups = [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] if with_counts: return [(group.grouper, group.list, len(group.list)) for group in groups] return groups @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, ...]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) groups = [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ] if with_counts: return [(group.grouper, group.list, len(group.list)) for group in groups] return groups

content.text: Now let's test this quickly.

input.command:
python -c "
from jinja2 import Environment
env = Environment()
tmpl = env.from_string('{% for city, items, count in users|groupby(\"city\", with_counts=True) %}{{ city }}:{{ count }} {% endfor %}')
users = [{'city': 'NY', 'name': 'a'}, {'city': 'NY', 'name': 'b'}, {'city': 'LA', 'name': 'c'}]
print(tmpl.render(users=users))

tmpl2 = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ city }}:{{ items|length }} {% endfor %}')
print(tmpl2.render(users=users))
"

LA:1 NY:2

file.content:
class _GroupTuple(t.NamedTuple): grouper: t.Any list: t.List # Use the regular tuple repr to hide this subclass if users print # out the value during debugging. def __repr__(self) -> str: return tuple.__repr__(self) def __str__(self) -> str: return tuple.__str__(self) @pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, <<<<<<< HEAD with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, ...]]": ======= case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": >>>>>>> team/agent1 """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
<<<<<<< HEAD You can pass ``with_counts=True`` to also get the number of items in each group, without needing ``|list|length`` in the template. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.2 Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. ======= Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will have the case of the first item in that group of values. For example, if a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the "CA" and "ca" entries will be grouped together. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", case_sensitive=true) %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. >>>>>>> team/agent1 .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ <<<<<<< HEAD expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) groups = [ ======= expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, default=default, ) out = [ >>>>>>> team/agent1 _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] <<<<<<< HEAD if with_counts: return [(group.grouper, group.list, len(group.list)) for group in groups] return groups ======= if not case_sensitive: # Return the real key from the first value instead of the lowercase key. output_expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = [_GroupTuple(output_expr(values[0]), values) for _, values in out] return out >>>>>>> team/agent1 @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, <<<<<<< HEAD with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, ...]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) groups = [ ======= case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, default=default, ) out = [ >>>>>>> team/agent1 _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ] <<<<<<< HEAD if with_counts: return [(group.grouper, group.list, len(group.list)) for group in groups] return groups ======= if not case_sensitive: # Return the real key from the first value instead of the lowercase key. output_expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = [_GroupTuple(output_expr(values[0]), values) for _, values in out] return out >>>>>>> team/agent1 @pass_environment def sync_do_sum( environment: "Environment", iterable: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None, start: V = 0, # type: ignore ) -> V: """Returns the sum of a sequence of numbers plus the value of parameter 'start' (which defaults to 0). When the sequence is empty it returns start. It is also possible to sum up only certain attributes: .. sourcecode:: jinja Total: {{ items|sum(attribute='price') }} .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The `attribute` parameter was added to allow suming up over attributes. Also the `start` parameter was moved on to the right. """ if attribute is not None: iterable = map(make_attrgetter(environment, attribute), iterable) return sum(iterable, start)

input.new_string:
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will have the case of the first item in that group of values. For example, if a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the "CA" and "ca" entries will be grouped together. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", case_sensitive=true) %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can pass ``with_counts=True`` to also get the number of items in each group, without needing ``|list|length`` in the template. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.2 Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, default=default, ) out = [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] if not case_sensitive: # Return the real key from the first value instead of the lowercase key. output_expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = [_GroupTuple(output_expr(values[0]), values) for _, values in out] if with_counts: return [(group.grouper, group.list, len(group.list)) for group in out] return out

input.new_string:
@async_variant(sync_do_groupby)  # type: ignore
async def do_groupby(
    environment: "Environment",
    value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]",
    attribute: t.Union[str, int],
    default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None,
    case_sensitive: bool = False,
    with_counts: bool = False,
) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, ...]]":
    expr = make_attrgetter(
        environment,
        attribute,
        postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None,
        default=default,
    )
    out = [
        _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values))
        for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr)
    ]

    if not case_sensitive:
        # Return the real key from the first value instead of the lowercase key.
        output_expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default)
        out = [_GroupTuple(output_expr(values[0]), values) for _, values in out]

    if with_counts:
        return [(group.grouper, group.list, len(group.list)) for group in out]

    return out

content.text: No conflict markers remain. Now let's test both features together.

input.command:
python -c "
from jinja2 import Environment
env = Environment()

users = [{'city': 'NY', 'name': 'a'}, {'city': 'ny', 'name': 'b'}, {'city': 'LA', 'name': 'c'}]

# default (case-insensitive) with counts
tmpl = env.from_string('{% for city, items, count in users|groupby(\"city\", with_counts=True) %}{{ city }}:{{ count }} {% endfor %}')
print(tmpl.render(users=users))

# case_sensitive with counts
tmpl2 = env.from_string('{% for city, items, count in users|groupby(\"city\", case_sensitive=True, with_counts=True) %}{{ city }}:{{ count }} {% endfor %}')
print(tmpl2.render(users=users))

# no counts, default behavior
tmpl3 = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ city }}:{{ items|length }} {% endfor %}')
print(tmpl3.render(users=users))
"

112 patch.txt diff --git a/src/jinja2/filters.py b/src/jinja2/filters.py index 80ea6504..7d8cbdb3 100644 --- a/src/jinja2/filters.py +++ b/src/jinja2/filters.py @@ -1163,7 +1163,9 @@ def sync_do_groupby( value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, -) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": + case_sensitive: bool = False, + with_counts: bool = False, +) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, ...]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, @@ -1203,18 +1205,60 @@ def sync_do_groupby(
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %} + Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is + case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will have + the case of the first item in that group of values. For example, if + a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the "CA" and + "ca" entries will be grouped together. + + .. sourcecode:: jinja + +
      {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", case_sensitive=true) %} +
    • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
      • + {% endfor %}
    + + You can pass ``with_counts=True`` to also get the number of items in + each group, without needing ``|list|length`` in the template. + + .. sourcecode:: jinja + +
      {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %} +
    • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
      • + {% endfor %}
    + + .. versionchanged:: 3.2 + Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. + + .. versionchanged:: 3.1 + Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. + .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ - expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) - return [ + expr = make_attrgetter( + environment, + attribute, + postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, + default=default, + )

content.content:
-    expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default)
-    return [
+    expr = make_attrgetter(
+        environment,
+        attribute,
+        postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None,
+        default=default,
+    )
+    out = [
         _GroupTuple(key, list(values))
         for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr)
     ]
 
+    if not case_sensitive:
+        # Return the real key from the first value instead of the lowercase key.
+        output_expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default)
+        out = [_GroupTuple(output_expr(values[0]), values) for _, values in out]
+
+    if with_counts:
+        return [(group.grouper, group.list, len(group.list)) for group in out]
+
+    return out
+

content.text:
Both agents have merged each other's work and confirmed all 837 tests pass. My `patch.txt` at `/workspace/repo/patch.txt` contains the full merged diff with both `case_sensitive` and `with_counts` parameters on `groupby`.

**Summary**: Added `with_counts` parameter to the `groupby` filter (`sync_do_groupby`/`do_groupby` in `src/jinja2/filters.py`), which yields `(grouper, items, count)` tuples when enabled. Coordinated with agent1 (adding `case_sensitive`) since both features touched the same functions — merged their branch, hand-resolved the conflict combining both params (order: `default, case_sensitive, with_counts`), verified all 837 tests pass, and pushed the final combined state to `team/agent2`.
